A.name: The Lead Leak
A.desc: Plain-English lead-flow angle. Paying $40-$75/click in DFW, but the lead form is broken or the phone is buried. You're buying traffic that never becomes a job. Three issues, tease the third. Close with calendar or sample audit.

beats.title: Lead Flow Leak
beats.line: Hi, my name is {caller} with RME Roofing — I review roofing company sites for a living. I pulled up your site, and your lead flow is costing you roughly $15,000 to $30,000 a week in roofing jobs. Do you know who I can talk to about this?
responses: What do you mean costing me?
beats.followup: Got it — here's what I'm seeing, then I'll show you exactly where the jobs are going.
beats.note: Calling the roofing owner, not a homeowner. No name tokens. No apology hedges. Dollar anchor = 1-3 missed jobs/wk × $10-18K each.

beats.title: Paying Per Click, Losing the Job
beats.line: Here's the deal — you're paying $40 to $75 per click in this market. That's real money for every homeowner who taps your ad. And right now, when they land on your site, a big chunk of them can't figure out how to reach you. No sticky phone number on mobile, form that requires too many steps, or a number that goes to voicemail. When did someone on your team last try to get a quote from your own site on a phone?
beats.followup: Yeah — that's what most owners say. The lead flow issue is just the first one. The bigger ones come after.
beats.note: Plain English. $40-$75/click anchor from DFW market_cpc.json. Ends with a question they can't answer well.

beats.title: The Three Leaks
beats.line: Here's what's actually happening. A homeowner with hail damage taps your ad, lands on your site, and can't find a clear way to call you — so they go to the next ad. You paid $40 to $75 for that click and never got a shot at the job. The ones who do stay hit your contact form and it's four fields before they've even decided. By the time they figure it out, they've opened two other roofers in other tabs. That's the $15,000 to $30,000 a week in lost jobs. Cleaning up the top of your page and making how they reach you obvious — those are week-one quick wins. But there's a third issue on your lead flow that costs more than both combined. I'd rather show you that one with your site open.
responses: How much would this cost to fix?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly what the audit answers. Pricing comes after.
beats.note: Tease the third issue. Do NOT reveal it on the call. The reveal is the audit. B2B framing — 'jobs' not 'bookings'.

beats.title: 100+ Roofing Sites, Same 3 Leaks
beats.line: I've done this on over 100 roofing sites — same 10-point checklist, same lead flow, same three leaks every time. The shops that fix the top two see their lead-to-call rate climb in the first week. The ones that fix all three usually double their close rate on paid traffic inside 30 days.
responses: How long does the fix take?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly why I lead with the audit, not the pitch.
beats.note: Credibility frame. 100+ sites. Same checklist. Pattern recognition. Roofing-owner language: lead-to-call rate, close rate.

beats.title: Free $3,000 Audit
beats.line: Here's what I want to do. I run a $3,000 conversion audit — and I'd like to do yours free. Thirty minutes. Your live site on screen. Every leak named in plain English, with dollar-anchored estimates per leak — what each one is costing you in jobs per week. You walk away with a sequenced one-week plan — every fix shipped at once, ranked by lead impact. Yours to keep whether you hire me to ship it or not. Not vibes — math.
beats.followup: No catch — I'm running a handful of these personally this quarter for case studies. That's the only ask.
beats.note: Verbatim voice from Conversion Exotics. $3K free. One-week plan. All fixes shipped at once.

beats.title: Calendar or Sample
beats.line: Two ways we can do this. I've got Thursday at 10 or Friday at 2 open — pick one and I'll send the calendar invite. Or, if you'd rather see what this looks like first, give me your email and I'll send over a sample audit I did for another roofing company — full plan, dollar numbers, the whole thing. Which one would you prefer?
beats.followup: Done — calendar invite or sample audit hits your inbox in 60 seconds.
beats.note: Dual-path close. Booked call OR sample audit. Either is a win. 'Roofing company' not 'rental shop'.

B.name: Next Contractor Steals It
B.desc: Side-by-side competitor steal angle. Homeowner has hail damage, taps your ad first, slow load or buried phone, taps next contractor. That guy gets the $15K insurance job. Same $15K-$30K/week anchor. Flip-the-steal audit framing.

beats.title: Next Contractor Steal
beats.line: Hi, my name is {caller} with RME Roofing — I review roofing company sites for a living. Quick question — do you know how many homeowners with hail damage tap your ad, count to three, and call the next contractor instead? Because on your site right now, that's costing you $15,000 to $30,000 a week in lost insurance jobs. Who's the right person to talk to about this?
responses: What do you mean costing me?
beats.followup: Got it — here's what I'm seeing, then I'll show you exactly where those jobs are going.
beats.note: Question-first hook lands the steal frame immediately. Insurance jobs framing is specific to roofing-owner language.

beats.title: Side-By-Side Speed Gap
beats.line: Here's what I did — I pulled up your site next to two other roofing shops in your market on my phone. Theirs loaded in about 2 to 3 seconds. Yours took closer to 6. That's the gap. Most homeowners give a site about 3 seconds before they back out and tap the next contractor — every extra second costs you roughly a third of your incoming leads. When's the last time someone on your team opened your site on a phone next to a competitor's?
beats.followup: Yeah — that's what most owners say. How fast it loads is just the first leak. The bigger ones come after.
beats.note: Side-by-side reframe makes the speed gap concrete. 'Leads' not 'bookings' — roofing-owner language.

beats.title: Next Contractor Gets The Job
beats.line: Here's what's actually happening. The homeowner has fresh hail damage. They Google, tap your ad, count to three — and the next shop's site is already loading. They tap it. They book the inspection. That contractor gets the $15,000 insurance job — you never even got on their screen. The ones who do stay land on a page that doesn't show proof of work, doesn't show a phone number they can tap, and asks them to fill out a form before they've decided anything. So they open two more tabs and forget you. That's the $15,000 to $30,000 a week. Fixing how fast it loads and making it obvious how to reach you — those are week-one quick wins. But there's a third issue on your lead flow that costs more than both combined. I'd rather show you that one with your site open.
responses: How much would this cost to fix?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly what the audit answers. Pricing comes after.
beats.note: Tease the third issue. Do NOT reveal it. Steal frame — 'next contractor' not 'next shop'. Insurance job framing.

beats.title: 100+ Roofing Sites Audited
beats.line: I've audited over 100 roofing sites — same 10-point checklist, same lead flow, same three leaks every time. The shops that fix the top two see their incoming leads climb in the first week. The ones that fix all three usually double their close rate on paid traffic inside 30 days.
responses: How long does the fix take?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly why I lead with the audit, not the pitch.
beats.note: Credibility frame. 100+ sites. Same checklist. Pattern recognition.

beats.title: Flip The Steal — Free $3,000 Audit
beats.line: Here's what I want to do. I run a $3,000 conversion audit — and I'd like to do yours free. Thirty minutes. Your live site on screen. I show you every leak, attach a dollar number to each one in jobs per week, and hand you a one-week plan to flip the steal — so the next time a homeowner with hail damage loads your ad side-by-side with a competitor, yours is the one they call. Yours to keep whether you hire me to ship it or not. Not vibes — math.
beats.followup: No catch — I'm running a handful of these personally this quarter for case studies. That's the only ask.
beats.note: Steal-frame hook ties audit back to Beat 3. One-week plan. Roofing language: jobs per week, hail damage.

beats.title: Calendar or Sample
beats.line: Two ways we can do this. I've got Thursday at 10 or Friday at 2 open — pick one and I'll send the calendar invite, and we'll get your site flipped from the one losing the click to the one closing the job. Or, if you'd rather see what this looks like first, give me your email and I'll send over a sample audit I did for another roofing company.
beats.followup: Done — calendar invite or sample audit hits your inbox in 60 seconds.
beats.note: Dual-path close. Steal-frame tilt on calendar path. 'Closing the job' — roofing-owner language.

C.name: 3-Issue Reveal
C.desc: Names two of three issues outright in plain English (top of page + how they reach you), teases the third for the audit. $15K-$30K/week anchor. Dual-path close.

beats.title: Three Issues Frame
beats.line: Hi, my name is {caller} with RME Roofing — I review roofing company sites for a living. I pulled up your site and I'm seeing three specific issues on your lead flow that together are costing you roughly $15,000 to $30,000 a week in roofing jobs. Do you know who I can talk to about this?
beats.followup: Got it — quick run-through of what I'm seeing, then I'll show you exactly where the jobs are going.
beats.note: Calling the roofing owner. Three-issues hook sets up the reveal. 'Jobs' not 'bookings'.

beats.title: Three Stuck Spots
beats.line: Here's the deal — every roofing site I look at has three spots where leads get stuck between tapping the ad and actually calling you: the top of your page, the part where they figure out how to reach you, and one more spot most owners never notice. On your site, all three are losing you jobs. You're paying $40 to $75 per click in this market — and for a big chunk of those clicks, the lead never turns into a call.
beats.followup: Yeah — that's the most common reaction. The reveal is in the audit.
beats.note: Plain-English version of CRO failure stack. No jargon. $40-$75 CPC anchor from DFW market_cpc.json. Tee up the reveal in Beat 3.

beats.title: Walk Through Two Of Three
beats.line: So here's what's actually happening. The top of your page doesn't show proof — no reviews, no jobs completed, no clear reason a homeowner with hail damage should pick you over the next contractor down the list. So they bounce. The ones who stay hit how you want them to reach you — and it's a form with four fields, or a phone number that's buried below the fold, or it goes straight to voicemail. That's a wall. They open two more tabs and forget you. That's the $15,000 to $30,000 a week in lost jobs. Those two are week-one quick wins. But there's a third issue on your lead flow that costs more than both of those combined. I'd rather show you that one with your site open.
responses: How much would this cost to fix?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly what the audit answers. Pricing comes after.
beats.note: Reveal two issues in plain English. Tease third. Do NOT name the third on the call — the reveal is the audit. Roofing-owner language throughout.

responses: How long does the fix take?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly why I lead with the audit, not the pitch.
beats.note: Credibility frame. 100+ sites. Same checklist. Pattern recognition.

beats.followup: No catch — I'm running a handful of these personally this quarter for case studies. That's the only ask.
beats.note: Verbatim voice from Conversion Exotics. $3K free. 'Jobs per week, not percentages' — roofing-owner language.

Which one would you prefer?
